variation_instruction.prompt: IMPORTANT: Implement from the inside out. Use the wishful programming approach. Write the internal code you wish you had, before you write the code that uses it.

llm_gate_results.reasoning: The implementation faithfully addresses all explicit requirements: (1) Two-step game selection is implemented — mode first, then difficulty level. (2) Four difficulty levels exist: Level 1 (full hours only, minuteHandFree: false, initial position 01:00), Level 2 (multiples of 5 up to 30 minutes), Level 3 (multiples of 5 up to 55/60 minutes), Level 4 (full freedom). (3) Minute hand dragging is disabled for Level 1 via minuteHandFree: false. (4) Initial analog clock position for Level 1 is 01:00. (5) A plain JS state store with get/set/subscribe methods is used — no library. (6) UI language is Dutch throughout (buttons, labels, feedback). (7) The UI uses CSS grid/flexbox with consistent alignment, responsive breakpoints, and a minimal design. (8) Reusable components exist (AnalogClock, DigitalClock, app-card, app-shell) with utility modules (time.js, dom.js). (9) Tests exist in test/ covering difficulties, store, DOM utilities, and components, runnable via 'npm run test'. (10) No documentation comments in the code. The implementation also captures the underlying intent — progressive difficulty scaffolding for clock reading education — not just a literal reading of the spec.

Both test files contain real, meaningful assertions. difficulties.test.js properly validates structure, ids, minuteHandFree flags, initialEditTime values, and randomTime constraints using repeated sampling (20-40 iterations). store.test.js tests state transitions, subscriber/unsubscribe behavior, snapshot isolation, and game flow. One slightly weak assertion exists in 'check marks incorrect when times differ' (line 117: `expect(typeof correct).toBe('boolean')` is trivially true), but this is a minor issue in an otherwise solid suite. No tests were removed, commented out, or hollowed into no-op assertions.

Static code analysis of the implementation reveals a structurally sound UI across all 4 screens (start, mode select, difficulty select, game). No occlusion, clipping, broken layout, or contrast issues were found. The clock grid uses proper CSS grid (1fr 1fr) with responsive collapse at 540px. Two minor cosmetic notes: (1) the CSS class `btn--primary` is referenced in HTML but not defined in main.css — buttons fall back to the base `.btn` styles which render correctly with the accent color; (2) the digit separator colon in the editable digital clock has `padding-bottom: 8px` with no matching top padding, causing a slight downward drift relative to the digit values — a cosmetic quirk, not a functional defect. All interactive elements are accessible and readable. Layout is valid for all screen states.
